grug.note: The grug voice is fun but I never actually feel violent about this stuff. The frustration is more of a slow burn — you watch the same anti-pattern play out at company after company and start to wonder if anyone reads postmortems.

This is actually the core of what I was trying to say, but grug-ifying it strips out the organizational nuance. It's not just about trust — it's about incentive structures that reward team-level metrics over company-level outcomes.

OK this one is genuinely true. I've watched a VP kill a monorepo proposal because of the word 'mono.' Branding matters more than technical merit in enterprise decisions, which is depressing but useful to know.

product.note: The PM framing immediately jumps to 'delivery velocity' which is fine but misses the human cost. Developers aren't just slower — they're demoralized by spending their days as code plumbers. That's a retention risk, not just a velocity metric.
This is correct but sanitized. 'Invisible friction' is PM-speak for 'your developers want to quit because they spend 40% of their time on yak-shaving that shouldn't exist.'
Reframing is the one thing the AI nailed across every version. I wish I'd been this explicit about it in my original post.

business.note: The business version immediately reaches for 'superlinear scaling' language that no exec I've worked with would actually use. They'd say 'it gets worse fast' and leave it at that.

This is the most AI-brained sentence in the whole set. 'Senior engineers spend a disproportionate amount of time' — yeah, we know. The question is whether leadership sees that as a problem or as the cost of doing business. Most choose the latter.

Invoking Google and Meta is the classic consultant move and it drives me nuts. Your 200-person company is not Google. The reasons Google uses a monorepo have almost nothing to do with why your team should.

engineering.note: Conway's Law is the right framing but the AI treats it as settled wisdom. In practice, I've seen plenty of teams where the repo structure doesn't mirror org structure at all — it mirrors whoever set up the repo three years ago and left the company.
This is the strongest paragraph across all versions and it's the one I wish I'd written. The build graph argument is genuinely compelling and I completely undersold it in my original.
CODEOWNERS as a governance mechanism sounds great in theory. In practice it becomes a bottleneck because the people listed as owners are always the busiest people on the team and reviews pile up.
